evidence_a: Canvas-design analysis: 'This skill is the static-design counterpart to the algorithmic-art skill, sharing the same philosophy-then-execute structure.' 'The agent first invents a named design movement manifesto, then renders it as a PDF or PNG.' Output is 'a single-page (or multi-page, if requested) visual artifact where text is sparse and design carries the meaning.'
evidence_b: Algorithmic-art analysis: 'This skill teaches an AI agent to generate interactive p5.js generative art through a two-phase creative pipeline: first, write a poetic algorithmic philosophy manifesto, then implement it as a living, parameterized visual system.' Output is 'a self-contained HTML artifact — seeded, tunable, reproducible — that works in any browser.'
rationale: Both skills solve the same core task: philosophy-first generative art creation where a manifesto precedes implementation. They differ in output format and interaction model — algorithmic-art produces interactive browser-based p5.js HTML artifacts with seed navigation and parameter sliders, while canvas-design produces static PDF/PNG visual designs. A pack synthesizer would choose one based on whether the use case demands an interactive explorable artifact or a static deliverable. The analyses explicitly identify each other as counterparts.
